preFix.title: Parsnip and curry soup
preFix.subtitle: Sweet, warming, and deeply spiced
preFix.excerpt: Roasted parsnips blended with a curry-spiced base, a little apple, and vegetable stock. The roasting concentrates the natural sweetness of the parsnips and gives the soup a depth that boiled parsnips cannot match.
preFix.sourceNotes: Curried parsnip soup is a British invention with no exact Indian precedent ,  part of the post-1970s tradition of applying Indian spicing to European root vegetables. A gastropub and dinner-party standard from the 1990s onwards.

content.text: Roasting is the step that makes this soup. Raw parsnips boiled directly in stock produce a pleasant but flat result; parsnips roasted at 200°C until their edges caramelise carry a sweetness and depth that survives blending. The curry powder is
in butter before the stock goes in, which rounds out the spice and removes any raw, dusty edge. Apple is optional but adds a brightness that lifts the whole soup.

Heat the oven to 200°C conventional. Spread the {{parsnip}} of parsnip chunks across a large roasting tin in a single layer. Drizzle with oil, season with salt, and toss to coat. Roast for 25 to 30 minutes until golden at the edges and tender throughout.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and {{ginger-fresh}} of grated ginger and cook for 1 minute.
Add {{curry-powder}} of curry powder and stir for 1 minute until fragrant. If using, add the {{apple}} of diced apple and stir for 30 seconds.
Add the roasted parsnips to the pan. Pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Simmer for 10 minutes to bring all the flavours together.
Blend until smooth with a stick blender. Taste and season. Thin with a little more stock if needed.
Ladle into bowls and add a spoonful of crème fraîche if using.

items.fix: Cook the curry powder for at least 1 full minute in the butter before adding anything else. The kitchen should smell fragrant and the spice colour should deepen slightly.
items.cause: The curry powder was not bloomed in fat long enough before liquid was added.

items.fix: Omit the apple, or add a squeeze of lemon juice to the finished soup. A small pinch of ground cumin or extra ginger helps balance sweetness.
items.cause: Parsnips are naturally sweet, especially when roasted, and the apple adds more.

items.fix: Remove the fibrous core from any parsnip thicker than about 3 cm before roasting. A jug blender gives a finer result than a stick blender.
items.cause: Larger parsnips have a woody core that does not blend well.

items.fix: Add hot stock a ladleful at a time until the consistency is right. A thick, velvety texture is the aim; too thin and the flavour dilutes.
items.cause: Parsnips are starchy and produce a naturally thick soup.

content.text: Curried parsnip soup has no Indian precedent. It is a British invention from the post-war period when curry powder became widely available in British supermarkets and cooks began using it as a general seasoning for root vegetables. By the 1990s it was a fixture of British gastropub menus ,  reliable, warming, and easy to make in large quantities. Despite its workaday reputation, when made with properly roasted parsnips and freshly ground spices, it is a very good soup.
